coredata.dc:title: Bright Blue-Light emitting cobalt doped CuS quantum dots: Photophysical studies and selective sensing application of ferric ion
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kalita, Mitul
Haque, Mahabul
Mondal, Amarjyoti
Singha Roy, Atanu
coredata.dc:description: Surface-functionalized quantum dots (QDs) have garnered significant attention in recent years for a variety of applications, including LEDs, photovoltaics, sensing, bioimaging and biomedical domains due to their distinct optical features such as strong photoluminescence behavior, high quantum yields etc. However, formation of nontoxic QDs is very challenging for the researchers because of their lower optical properties as compared to Cd based QDs. The phenomenon of doping in semiconductor QDs is an effective way to achieve high opto-electrical properties in the host QDs. We have presented a low temperature colloidal synthesis of CuS QDs using cobalt (Co2+) as a doping agent with an exceptional stability at room temperature for 30 days. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of Co2+-doped CuS QDs exhibit a deep-blue emission at 420 nm resulting in excellent optical property with an improvement of photoluminescence quantum yield. Due to remarkable CIE chromaticity coordinates, good CCT values, and high colour purity, the synthesized Co2+-doped CuS QDs could be used extensively in LEDs and prove to be useful as blue phosphors. The synthesized Co2+-doped CuS QDs also act as a fluorescence probe in the detection of ferric ion (Fe3+) with high sensitivity, good selectivity, a low limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ), of (4.99 ± 0.12) μM and (16.67 ± 0.40) μM, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Blue emitting
Fe3+ sensing
Fluorescence
LEDs
Quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of undoped and cobalt doped CuS QDs
Analysis of optical and structural properties of the synthesized QDs
Fluorescence response of Fe3+ ion to the synthesized Co2+-doped CuS QDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: MK expresses gratitude to NIT Meghalaya for the research fellowship. The authors thank CIF NIT Meghalaya for the PXRD analysis. The authors would also like to thank SAIF CSIR-NEIST, Jorhat for XPS analysis. The author would also like to thank IASST, Guwahati, India, for DLS, \u03B6-potential, PL lifetime, TEM and FESEM analyses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL spectra, (b) UV–visible spectra, (c) Tauc plots, (d) PXRD patterns, zoomed one in (e) and (f) decay of PL intensity of undoped and cobalt doped CuS QDs respectively.
High resolution XPS spectra of 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs.

TEM images with size distribution histogram from the TEM analysis, hydrodynamic size histogram from DLS measurement, FESEM and EDX images of the undoped CuS QDs (a, b, c, g) and 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs (d, e, f, h), respectively.

(a) Fluorescence quenching profiles of 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, (b) linear correlation graph of Fe3+ (0-5μΜ), (c) PL decay of 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ and (d) Selectivity of different metal ions.
(a) Circuit diagram of low cost simple LED fabrication, (b) image of fabricated blue QLEDs, (c) CIE color coordinates of respected blue QLEDs and (d) Energy level diagram of cobalt doped CuS QDs.
Synthesis schemes of cobalt doped CuS QDs.
The LOD and LOQ values for the detection of Fe3+ using 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs as sensing probes.
Lifetime decay parameters of undoped and 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs and complexes with Fe3+ ions.

head.ce:title: Bright Blue-Light emitting cobalt doped CuS quantum dots: Photophysical studies and selective sensing application of ferric ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Sciences, National Institute of Technology Meghalaya Shillong, 793003, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Sciences
National Institute of Technology Meghalaya Shillong

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Sciences, National Institute of Technology Meghalaya Shillong, 793003, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface-functionalized quantum dots (QDs) have garnered significant attention in recent years for a variety of applications, including LEDs, photovoltaics, sensing, bioimaging and biomedical domains due to their distinct optical features such as strong photoluminescence behavior, high quantum yields etc. However, formation of nontoxic QDs is very challenging for the researchers because of their lower optical properties as compared to Cd based QDs. The phenomenon of doping in semiconductor QDs is an effective way to achieve high opto-electrical properties in the host QDs. We have presented a low temperature colloidal synthesis of CuS QDs using cobalt (Co2+) as a doping agent with an exceptional stability at room temperature for 30 days. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of Co2+-doped CuS QDs exhibit a deep-blue emission at 420 nm resulting in excellent optical property with an improvement of photoluminescence quantum yield. Due to remarkable CIE chromaticity coordinates, good CCT values, and high colour purity, the synthesized Co2+-doped CuS QDs could be used extensively in LEDs and prove to be useful as blue phosphors. The synthesized Co2+-doped CuS QDs also act as a fluorescence probe in the detection of ferric ion (Fe3+) with high sensitivity, good selectivity, a low limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ), of (4.99 ± 0.12) μM and (16.67 ± 0.40) μM, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: A lot of attention has been focused on colloidal quantum dots (QDs) as the most promising options for next-generation solid-state lighting and display materials due to their intriguing characteristics, which include size-dependent emission, high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), high color purity, and persistent photostability [1,2]. When QDs are doped with lanthanide or transition metal ions (e.g., Co2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Ni2+, etc.), the overlap between the wave functions of photogenerated electrons and holes is disrupted, which results in longer exciton/carrier lifetimes and much larger photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) [3–8]. However, the transfer of research findings to practical applications is significantly hindered by the presence of harmful heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg etc.). Hence, heavy metal-free semiconductor QDs have recently gained popularity [9,10]. The deep blue and near UV (400 nm and below) regions of the spectrum, however, have very few examples despite the wide range of materials available. These efficient, wide band gap UV emitters are crucial for many applications, including photocatalysis, biotechnology, and UV-LEDs [11]. Doped QDs are therefore good candidates for optoelectronic applications since the carrier extraction is made simple [12–14]. Doping can minimize cytotoxicity in QDs, enhance emission lifespan, change optical and electrical properties to the point where the doped material is regarded as a novel luminous material [15,16]. Doped semiconductor QDs have been identified as a possible source of luminescence due to their dopants acting as luminescence centres and creating deep trap levels. The features of host materials, such as long excited state lifetimes for luminescence, large Stokes shifts, thermally stable luminescence, tunable emission colours, and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), can be altered by doping of semiconductor QDs with impurities [17]. When the surface energy is altered by doping, semiconductor QDs crystal formation may also be impacted. As a result, the QDs' growth and morphology could be impacted [18–22]. Recently, cobalt (Co2+) has been employed as a dopant in numerous research to improve QYs and expand the emission zone of NCs. In contrast to Mn, Cu, Ni-doped NCs, Co doping is still relatively recent considering its potential to be used in optoelectronic devices since it is a good luminescence activator and provides good traps for excited electrons [23,24].

Cobalt, an environmentally friendly transition metal ion, is a good choice for its use in LEDs because of several benefits, which include inexpensive raw materials, simplicity in acquisition, and excellent photoluminescence characteristics [25]. In a Co2+-doped CuS system we observed that there is a dominant blue emission present at 420 nm mainly due to the 4A2 to 4T1 transition of the Co2+ impurity excited by energy transfer from the host lattice [26]. CuS is a good choice for a variety of applications in fields like dye-sensitized solar cells, solar control coatings, lithium ion batteries, photocatalysis, photodegradation, gas sensors, optical filters, high temperature thermistors, photothermal therapy, biosensing and drug delivery because of its non-toxic nature, low synthesis cost, stoichiometric composition, and multi-functionality [27–30]. Doped semiconductor nanocrystals produced by colloidal synthesis, which has already shown itself to be an intriguing area for the upcoming nanotechnology applications, since it can produce emission sources that are highly efficient for a variety of utilizations, is one path that offers the possibility of obtaining such materials. Even with numerous attempts to grow doped QDs in solution, doping all the nanocrystals in the reaction mixture at the same time with different dopants continues to be difficult because the host matrix tends to “self-purify” by pushing the dopant ions from the surface of the interior crystal structure. Consequently, it is still challenging to successfully dope a host nanocrystal by adding a small quantity of dopant precursors during the synthesis process, even in the best-case scenario when the dopant ions have a similar valence state with the same ionic radius as the respective host. To get around this, a variety of doping techniques, including nucleation-doping and growth-doping, offer plenty of opportunities to introduce dopants selectively in the host materials at suitable locations to produce distinct emission centers within a single quantum dot molecule.

Ferric ion (Fe3+) is essential to numerous physiological processes in the body. Whether the level of Fe3+ is too high or too low, it will impact metabolism and cell homeostasis, which can result in major illnesses like diabetes, skin conditions, anaemia, heart failure, harm to the brain and behaviour, reduced immunity, renal failure etc. Therefore, it is practically important to create a simple and quick method for identifying Fe3+ ion in water. Many methods, including atomic absorption/emission spectrometry [31], cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry, high-performance liquid chromatography [32], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and atomic fluorescence spectrometry [33], are used to detect Fe3+ ions from various origins. Unfortunately, because of the high cost of the equipment, the need for a skilled operator, and the difficulty of sample preparation, these are not suitable for small-scale companies. Consequently, the photoluminescence method is one of the most widely utilized sensing techniques because of its high sensitivity and exceptional adaptability. Fluorescence based sensing techniques have advanced significantly in recent times, yet a major issue still remains: ambient interference causes many fluorescent probes to yield false-positive results [34]. To resolve this problem, a new fluorescence probe called QDs might be utilized. We must thus continue to produce Fe3+ biosensors with a wide detection range and very low detection limit utilizing non-toxic Co2+-doped CuS QDs.

In this work, we have reported Co2+-doped CuS QDs that emit blue light without using toxic elements and described their optical, morphological, and structural characteristics using a simple co-precipitation approach. The Co2+-doped CuS QDs is used to create a phosphor converted light emitting diode (pc-LED), which produces stable blue light at ∼ 10 V. The nontoxic as synthesized QDs also show outstanding sensitivity and selectivity when probing the Fe3+ ion. Overall, doping Co2+ ions could significantly enhance the optical performance of CuS QDs, indicating that this phosphor has a great deal of potential advancement for blue light illumination as well as expanding its application in metal ion sensing.

CuCl2 (98 %, C3714) and Methyl Methacrylate Polymer were procured from TCI (Japan), Na2S·9H2O (99.9 %, 90945) and 4-Mercaptophenylboronic acid (MBA) (90 %, 524018) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA., Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O (98 %, 10218576) was obtained from Alfa Aesar (USA), toluene (99.5 %, 17213) was a product from HIMEDIA, hydrazine hydrate (80 %, 53931), anhydrous NaH2PO4 (99 %, 59443), anhydrous Na2HPO4 (99 %, 21669) and FeCl3 (98 %, 72287) were received from SRL (Sisco Research Laboratories), India. All of the reagents were utilized without any further purification exactly as supplied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of undoped and cobalt doped CuS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The conventional co-precipitation method was adopted to synthesize undoped and doped QDs as presented in the scheme 1 . For undoped host, 2 mM Cu(CH3COO)2·5H2O, 2 mM MBA were dissolved in 6 mL double distilled water followed by the addition of hydrazine hydrate and heated at 70 °C under inert atmosphere. After 20 mins later 1 mM Na2S was added and again heated for 2 h. The synthesized QDs were obtained by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min. A similar method was used to synthesize 2 %, 5 % and 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs using the same methods with a slight modification of the addition of Co2+ dopant precursors in the reaction mixture containing the host precursors.

Briefly, a variety of spectral methods were employed to characterize the synthesized undoped and Co2+-doped CuS QDs. Using PerkinElmer Lambda 35 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer, the optical absorption characteristics of the synthesised CuS QDs were characterized within the wavelength range of 300–700 nm. A Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Scientific, Japan) was used to record the emission spectra of QDs in the range of 360–550 nm upon excitation at a wavelength of 290 nm. Excited state fluorescence lifetime experiments were carried out on the TCSPC instrument (LifeSpec II model: FSP 920, Edinburg Instrument, UK). The purity, phase and crystallinity of the synthesised CuS QDs were ascertained by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data. The diffraction patterns were measured on a Rigaku SmartLab SE PXRD instrument (Rigaku Corporation, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm and 2θ value 20°-70°). The elemental composition and surface morphology of the synthesized CuS QDs were investigated using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) from Carl Zeiss, Sigma 300 VP. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) equipment from Thermo Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd., UK (Model: ESCALAB Xi + ) was used to analyze the surface composition of the synthesised CuS QDs. The size, shape, and SAED patterns of the CuS QDs particles were measured using a 200 kV voltage-operated TEM instrument JEOL (JEM-2100). TEM micrographs were recorded after the CuS QDs solution was drop-cast onto a copper grid to prepare the samples. Using Zeta Sizer (Model: Nano ZS90, Malvern Analytical) analysis of the size and stability of the Co2+-doped CuS QDs was confirmed. The surface functionalization of CuS QDs was measured in 4000–400 cm−1 range using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (PerkinElmer Spectrum Two instrument UATR IR instrument).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of optical and structural properties of the synthesized QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesized undoped and Co2+-doped CuS QDs are characterized by various spectral techniques (details procedure of characterization techniques are elucidated in the section 2.3). Concerning the emission properties, it is evident from Fig. 1 (a) and Fig. S3 that with increase in percentage of Co2+-doped, the intensity of PL spectra increases and shows consistent redshift of 20 nm in the PL spectra. From Fig. 1(b), the absorption band edge of synthesized QDs are found between ∼320-420 nm. Using the Tauc equation (Eq. (3.1)) [35], the band gap of the synthesised CuS QDs was determined from the absorption spectrum.
(3.1)
α h υ
=
A ( h υ -
E g
)
r
In this equation, α, E g, hυ, and A represent the absorption coefficient, bandgap energy, photon energy and constant, respectively, with r equal to 1/2. Fig. 1(c) shows the Tauc plot that was used to estimate the band gap for several important CuS-based samples. With the increase in concentration of cobalt (Co2+) doping, band gap of the synthesized CuS QDs decreases from 3.63 eV to 3.36 eV. Which may be due to exchange interaction of d-electrons (Co2+) with valence and conduction band electrons of host QDs. Fig. S2 shows the linear decrease of band gap which can be attributed to the band electrons and localized d electrons of the Co2+ sp-d exchange interaction. Additionally, the charge imbalance between the Cu and Co cations forms sulphur vacancies, which may also cause the band gap to vary. Therefore the bandgap of CuS QDs can be tuned with Co2+ doping [36]. Again, the application of QDs requires long-term colloidal and optical stability which is frequently limited by the alteration or deterioration of the QDs’ surface. Both undoped and Co2+-doped QDs have good colloidal stability over a period of one month as determined by the evolution of photoluminescence spectra of both undoped and Co2+-doped CuS QDs for a month while keeping them under normal conditions (Fig. S7 (b)).

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns for CuS QDs synthesised at 70 °C with varying concentrations of Co2+ dopant are displayed in Fig. 1(d). All patterns can be indexed according to the reference obtained from JCPDS card no. 060464, confirms hexagonal crystalline CuS QDs [37]. When Co2+-doping is increased, there is a noticeable shift in the peaks towards smaller angles and a rise in peak broadening, as can be seen by closely examining the XRD diffraction patterns in Fig. 1(e). The Crystallite domain size of synthesised undoped and Co2+-doped CuS QDs was determined using the Debye-Scherrer equation [38] (Eq. (3.2)) as follows.
(3.2)
L =
O . 9 λ
β c o s θ
where L and λ are the crystallite length and wavelength of the X-ray source, respectively. In PXRD spectra, β represents the full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the (110) crystalline plane, whereas θ represents the Bragg's angle (in radians).

Results from Scherrer analysis showed that for all the prepared samples, there is a progressive decrease in crystallite size from 3.81 to 3.11 nm with increasing Co2+-doping (Fig. S4). The nucleation and growth processes of CuS QDs are thus clearly perturbed by Co2+ dopants.

The time-correlated single photon count (TCSPC) analysis also revealed the formation of undoped and Co2+-doped CuS QDs (Table 2). Fig. 1(f) shows that the average lifetimes of undoped CuS and 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs were determined to be 2.32 ± 0.12 and 1.43 ± 0.29 ns, respectively. 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs exhibit faster short-term decay of blue emission compared to undoped CuS QDs, indicating an extra decay pathway for surface states through energy transfer to dopant levels [39].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) is a useful tool for determining the functional groups present in QDs or other nanomaterials. Fig. S7(d) displays the FT-IR spectra of undoped and Co2+-doped CuS QDs. The capping agent MBA shows native characteristics frequencies at 3370 cm−1 for B-OH stretching vibrations indicating distinct boronic acid environments where intramolecular hydrogen bonding takes place [40], 3033 cm−1 corresponds to C-H stretching vibrations of phenyl ring, 2569 cm−1 for S-H stretching frequency [41], 1600 cm−1 corresponds to aromatic C = C stretching frequency, 1332 cm−1 represents the O-H vibrations in plane, 996 cm−1 corresponds to B-C vibrations and 828 cm−1 represents B-O stretching vibrations [42]. In CuS and Co2+-doped CuS QDs, the peak at about 690 cm−1 was located, which fingerprints the metal-chalcogenide (Cu-S bond) stretching frequency indicating the formation of CuS QDs [43]. Moreover, the S-H peak disappeared in QDs indicating the involvement of −SH group in the surface-functionalization of the respective QDs. Significant differences in presence of other functional groups were also observed (Fig. S7(d)) These findings suggested that the functional groups of MBA play a crucial role in the formation of respective MBA capped nano-sized CuS and Co2+-doped CuS QDs.

The composition of the QDs and the chemical states of the constituent components have been examined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The synthesized undoped and Co2+- doped CuS QDs samples contain all of the expected elements, as shown by a survey scan. Fig. S6 and Fig. 2 represent the high-resolution XPS spectra of both undoped and 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs. In addition, the spectra have been deconvoluted with respect to the spin–orbit splitting, where standard values from the database are used to correct the binding energy differences, full width at half maximum (FWHM), and area under the curve. From Fig. S6 the distinct peaks could be seen in the spectra of Cu 2p corresponding to Cu 2p3/2 (932.74 eV), and 2p1/2 (952.35 eV), and S 2p3/2 (163.01 eV) and 2p1/2 (161.83 eV) due to spin–orbit peaks further confirms the formation of CuS QDs [37]. The deconvoluted spectrum of C 1 s, O 1 s and B 1 s, respectively, confirm the formation of CuS QDs (C-C, C-O, C = O and C-OH functional groups, respectively). The XPS spectra of 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs are depicted in the Fig. 2. The Cu 2p spectral region shows two symmetric peaks at 932.67 and 952.32 eV, corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively. The peaks observed at 163.01 and 161.7 eV are related to the spin–orbit interactions of S 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively. The presences of two peaks at 781.88 and 798.01 eV, corresponds to Co 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 respectively [44]. These results indicate the formation of the Co2+-doped CuS QDs. The other peaks were observed for C 1 s and O 1 s respectively, which suggested the presences of C-C, C-O, C = O etc., functional groups present in the Co2+-doped CuS QDs. These results are consistent with the outcomes of the FT-IR and EDX investigations.

From Fig. 3 for both undoped and 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs, the respective average grain sizes as determined by the TEM measurements are found to be 3.72 ± 0.01 nm and 3.14 ± 0.03 nm, respectively. These values are consistent with the DLS measurement and PXRD data. Using a field emission electron microscope (FESEM), the surface shape and composition of the synthesised Co2+-doped CuS QDs were examined. CuS QDs with partial agglomeration are depicted in Fig. 3(c, f), where their smooth shape and spherical size are displayed. The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra Fig. 3(g, h) showed strong absorption peaks for the elements copper and sulphur, further supporting the production of both undoped and 10 % Co2+-doped CuS nanocrystals. The peaks for C, O and B are due to the presence of capping agent 4-Mercaptophenylboronic acid used in the as synthesis of QDs.

By using DLS experiments hydrodynamic radii of both undoped and 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs were calculated to be 3.96 ± 0.95 nm and 3.16 ± 0.10 nm respectively. From the Table S4 highly polydispersed 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs have higher magnitude of ζ-potential value than the undoped CuS QDs. The ζ-potential measures the degree of electrostatic repulsion between nearby comparable charged particles in a dispersed system. The negative ζ-potential indicates that electrostatic forces between molecules are repulsive. The repulsive force among molecules increases as the negative charge increases [45]. Furthermore, dispersion of CuS QDs has been observed to increase the numerical values of ζ-potential in dispersed systems containing Co2+-doped CuS QDs. This work shows that studying ζ-potential gives significant information about the stability and aggregation of Co2+-doped CuS QDs distributed nematic systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Fe3+ ion to the synthesized Co2+-doped CuS QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The Fe3+ concentration-dependent quenching of 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs fluorescence was monitored. Due to the strong biochemical interaction between Fe3+ and the 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs probe, a suppressing pattern of the corresponding synthesized Co2+-doped CuS QDs fluorescence was seen with the rise of Fe3+ concentration (0–5 μM) shown in Fig. 4 . A linear Stern-Volmer plot indicated the involvement of a single kind of quenching mechanism. For Fe3+ sensing linear correlation plots (R2 = 0.998) obtained by analyzing the quenching patterns of the respective QDs fluorescence intensities using the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (3.3)) shown below.
(3.3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]
where F 0 , F, K SV, and Q symbolize the fluorescence intensities of synthesized 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs in the absence of Fe3+, fluorescence intensities at varying concentrations of Fe3+, the slope of the correlation curve, and concentrations of Fe3+, respectively, kq and τ0 are the bimolecular quenching constant and fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore probes, respectively. The sensing parameters were determined by calculating the slope of the linear Stern-Volmer plot, also known as the calibration plot. Eq. (3.4) and Eq. (3.5) were used to assess the CuS QDs sensor's capacity to detect Fe3+ in terms of LOD and LOQ values. The findings are shown in the Table 1
.
(3.4)
LOD =
3 σ
k
(3.5)
LOQ =
10 σ
k
where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurement (n = 10) and k is the slope of the calibration curve.

The LOD and LOQ values found to be 4.99 ± 0.12 μM and 16.67 ± 0.40 μM, respectively. For 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs, the LOD value obtained was superior to that found in published literatures [46,47]. Static and dynamic quenching are two terms used to describe the quenching mechanisms, which can be better understood by absorption studies, temperature dependent quenching studies, bimolecular quenching constant (k q) values, and more precisely, fluorescence lifetime analysis [48].

In aqueous medium at 298 K, the 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs in presence of Fe3+ ion have k q values higher than the maximum diffusion control value (k diff.: 2.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1) indicating a static quenching mechanism was involved in the interaction processes (Table S3) [49]. Time-resolve fluorescence lifetime measurement (SI, section 1.3) of the synthesised QDs and their complexes was used to confirm the exact quenching mechanism type. This is an accurate and reliable method for determining the quenching mechanism. However, fluorescence lifetime measurement contradicted the results obtained from the steady state fluorescence data. The average lifetime of the synthesized 10 % Co2+-doped CuS QDs was observed as 1.43 ± 0.29 ns, which in presence of Fe3+ ions decreased to 1.15 ± 0.12 ns, indicating the involvement of dynamic quenching in the complex formation [50]. Hence, we can conclude that a combined type of mechanism i.e. static and dynamic quenching both were involved in the interaction between Co2+-doped CuS QDs and Fe3+ ions.

In conclusion, we have successfully synthesized toxic metal free undoped and Co2+-doped CuS QDs using co-precipitation method. The significant luminescence features of the synthesised undoped and Co2+-doped CuS QDs were emphasized by the fluorescence emission data, bandgap measurements, fluorescence lifetime studies, and UV–visible spectral recording. The formation and efficient surface passivation of synthesised QDs were further supported by FT-IR, FESEM, EDX, and XPS analyses. An increase in the optical characteristics (such as QY and photoluminescence emission) results in due to the formation of Co2+-doped CuS QDs. Detailed PXRD analyses revealed that the synthesized Co2+-doped CuS QDs exhibited hexagonal crystalline structure. These phosphors exhibit remarkable sensitivity and good selectivity towards Fe3+ ions sensing with LOD and LOQ values of 4.99 ± 0.12 μΜ and 16.67 ± 0.40 μM respectively, in addition to their excellent CIE chromaticity coordinates and high colour purity measured upto ∼ 86 %. Combined type of mechanism i.e. static and dynamic quenching was involved in the interaction between Co2+-doped CuS QDs and Fe3+ ion, as demonstrated by the result obtained from steady state fluorescence data and fluorescence lifetime analysis. The synthesized Co2+-doped CuS QDs might be effectively used to fabricate reliable, vivid blue QD-LEDs at a reasonable cost using simple LED fabrication method.

Mitul Kalita: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Mahabul Haque: Methodology, Investigation. Amarjyoti Mondal: Methodology, Investigation. Atanu Singha Roy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: MK expresses gratitude to NIT Meghalaya for the research fellowship. The authors thank CIF NIT Meghalaya for the PXRD analysis. The authors would also like to thank SAIF CSIR-NEIST, Jorhat for XPS analysis. The author would also like to thank IASST, Guwahati, India, for DLS, ζ-potential, PL lifetime, TEM and FESEM analyses.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Details of QY determination, data and plot related to XPS, AAS analyses and LED fabrication are included.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.116137.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Augmented band gap tunability in indium-doped zinc sulfide nanocrystals

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blue-emitting InP quantum dots participate in an efficient resonance energy transfer process in water
